\section{Problem}
Throughout the continuous lifecycle of software maintenance and evolution, systems inevitably suffer from architectural drift as the implemented codebase gradually diverges from its prescriptive design constraints. This degradation is introduced by both human developers through ad hoc modifications and by AI coding agents that generate locally correct but structurally harmful code due to their reliance on localized context windows. While recent advances have enabled automated architecture recovery from source code with high accuracy, these approaches remain reactive. They reconstruct architecture after drift has already occurred but provide no mechanism to prevent it from recurring. This project aims to close that gap by automatically translating recovered architectures into enforceable build-time constraints that block violations before they are merged into production.

\section{Motivation}
Addressing architectural drift proactively is critical because unchecked structural degradation quietly accumulates massive technical debt that ultimately paralyzes a system's capacity for long-term maintenance and evolution. This challenge grows more urgent as development teams increasingly adopt autonomous AI coding agents that lack awareness of system-level design constraints. Software architects and maintenance engineers will directly benefit from this research by gaining an automated enforcement pipeline that continuously validates proposed changes against the intended architecture. Consequently, development teams can integrate both human contributors and AI agents into their workflows while preserving the structural integrity and sustainability of their complex systems.

\section{Previous Work}
Recent literature has increasingly explored the intersection of LLMs and software architecture, but efforts to use AI for detecting architectural drift have revealed significant limitations.
\subsection{Exploring Architectural Smells Detection Through LLMs}
Tessa et al.~\cite{tessa2025} investigated the use of off-the-shelf LLMs to detect structural symptoms of drift, such as architectural smells. Specifically, they evaluated Google's Gemini 1.5 Pro on its ability to detect Hub-like Dependencies (HL) in open-source Java projects, comparing its performance against Arcan, a specialized architectural smell detection tool. An HL is a critical smell where a component has excessive incoming and outgoing dependencies.
The study revealed a severe precision and explainability gap. While the LLM achieved 100\% recall in spotting high-dependency components, its precision varied significantly. More detailed prompts improved detection performance from 64\% to 82\% for lower-severity smells, yet the model still could not reliably distinguish architecturally significant dependencies from benign ones. Furthermore, only 49\% of the LLM's explanations for the detected smells were deemed satisfactory by human evaluators. This indicates that the model relies on superficial pattern matching rather than genuine architectural reasoning.

\subsection{Towards Automated Identification of Violation Symptoms of Architecture Erosion}
Li et al.~\cite{li2023} explored the automated identification of architecture erosion by analyzing developer discussions in code reviews. They developed 15 machine learning-based and 4 deep learning-based classifiers using three pre-trained word embeddings to detect violation symptoms from code review comments across four large open-source projects: OpenStack Nova, OpenStack Neutron, Qt Base, and Qt Creator. They further compared these traditional classifiers against LLMs such as GPT-4o, Qwen-2.5, and DeepSeek-R1. The best performing traditional classifier was SVM with word2vec, achieving an F1-score of 0.779.
This approach is fundamentally reactive and dependent on human oversight. It only succeeds when a developer has already noticed the violation and discussed it in a code review comment. Because the classifiers evaluate natural language discussions rather than the underlying structural graph of the codebase, they remain entirely blind to silent drift, which refers to subtle architectural violations that bypass human reviewers during routine feature updates and are never documented in any textual artifact.

\subsection{ArchAgent: Scalable Legacy Software Architecture Recovery with LLMs}
In one of the most advanced attempts to handle large-scale systems, Pan et al.~\cite{pan2026} introduced ArchAgent, a hybrid framework designed to recover architectural design from legacy software. It combines traditional static analysis using Abstract Syntax Trees (ASTs) with LLM-powered semantic synthesis to reconstruct architecture diagrams from cross-repository codebases. In their evaluation across eight large-scale GitHub projects, ArchAgent achieved a mean F1 score of 0.966, demonstrating that accurate architecture recovery from source code is feasible.

However, the authors explicitly highlight the local-scope bias and context limitations of modern LLMs as a primary hurdle. Industrial codebases frequently exceed millions of tokens, which overwhelms the context windows of even the most advanced models. More critically, ArchAgent treats recovery as a one-time diagnostic event. Once the architecture is reconstructed, no mechanism exists to enforce it against future changes, leaving the recovered architecture vulnerable to the same drift that necessitated its reconstruction.

\subsection{Formal Software Architecture Rule Learning: A Comparative Investigation between Large Language Models and Inductive Techniques}
Schindler and Rausch \cite{schindler2024} investigate the use of machine learning techniques to automatically infer software architecture rules from implementation data, addressing the limitations of manual rule specification, which is often time-consuming and error-prone. The study compares inductive rule learning (IRL) approaches with LLMs by applying both to a knowledge base derived from real-world software systems. Their results show that IRL methods, particularly tools like Popper, are generally more effective and consistent in learning rules that align with predefined architectural hypotheses and demonstrate reasonable generalizability across system subsets. In contrast, LLMs exhibit limitations, struggling with formal rule induction, especially when semantic cues are reduced or negative examples are introduced, though they can infer simple rules under favorable conditions. Overall, the paper highlights both the promise and current constraints of data-driven approaches for architectural rule discovery, suggesting that while ML techniques can support more adaptive software architecture practices, further research is needed to improve their robustness and scalability.

Collectively, these studies demonstrate that current approaches face two compounding limitations. First, AI models cannot reliably detect architectural drift natively. They evaluate code locally rather than globally, lack the semantic business context required to distinguish intended design from implemented design, and produce high false-positive rates when evaluating complex structural patterns. Second, even when recovery succeeds with high accuracy, no existing approach translates the recovered architecture into automated, continuous enforcement. Architecture recovery remains a one-time reconstruction rather than an ongoing safeguard, leaving systems vulnerable to recurring drift. This project addresses that gap. While our implementation builds on the ArchAgent repository, it extends prior work, particularly the study by Schindler and Rausch \cite{schindler2024}, by moving beyond rule inference toward integrating learned architectural rules into a continuous enforcement pipeline, bridging the gap between one-time discovery and sustained architectural compliance.


\section{Solution}
We propose an automated enforcement pipeline that translates recovered software architectures into executable build-time constraints, see Figure~\ref{fig:solution}. The pipeline takes an architecture representation and a project package structure. It prompts an LLM to generate enforceable rules in the native format of deterministic tools. For Java repositories, it generates ArchUnit test suites. For Go repositories, it generates arch-go configurations. This extends the rule-learning approach of Schindler and Rausch \cite{schindler2024} by operationalizing learned constraints as continuously executable checks. The goal is to prevent architectural drift before it is merged into production rather than recovering architecture reactively. The LLM acts as a translator. ArchUnit and arch-go act as deterministic judges.

\begin{figure*}[t]
    \centering
    \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{cs6356 solution.png}
    \caption{Workflow Diagram.}
    \label{fig:solution}
\end{figure*}

\subsubsection{Input}
\begin{itemize}
    \item Target software repositories
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Java: Spring Framework and Apache ZooKeeper
        \item Go: HashiCorp Consul
    \end{itemize}
    \item Ground truth architecture documentation provided by the ArchAgent benchmark for each target repository
    \item Extracted package directory structures and package structure listings from the source tree
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection{Output}
\begin{itemize}
    \item Executable enforcement artifacts that encode the intended architecture as deterministic dependency constraints
    \item Java artifacts
    \begin{itemize}
        \item ArchUnit test suite
        \item Standalone Maven test harness
    \end{itemize}
    \item Go artifacts
    \begin{itemize}
        \item arch-go YAML rule file
        \item Standalone Makefile that runs \texttt{arch-go check}
    \end{itemize}
    \item Supporting reports for review and analysis that record compilation fixes, adversarial review findings, and violation triage results
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection{Algorithm and Data Transformations}
\begin{enumerate}
    \item Input collection: Collect the ground truth architecture documentation and the extracted package structure for the target repository.
    \item Rule generation: Provide the documentation and package structure to an LLM and generate enforcement artifacts. Java outputs are ArchUnit test suites and a standalone Maven test harness. Go outputs are arch-go YAML rules and a standalone Makefile.
    \item Compilation fix loop: Execute the generated artifacts. If the build fails, provide the compilation errors to the model and regenerate the artifacts. Record each correction in the fix history.
    \item Adversarial review and patching: Send the generated rules to a reviewer model for adversarial review against the same documentation. Use \texttt{claude-opus-4-7} as the reviewer model. Apply the recommended patches and record the changes in the fix history.
    \item Baseline validation and triage loop: Run the rules against the repository baseline and collect violations. Use the violation analysis prompt to triage each violation as a mapping error or a real violation using the ground truth documentation. Patch mapping errors and rerun until mapping errors reach zero.
    \item Calibration: Use the final thoughts prompt to write a final alignment pass. State what the passing rules prove. State what remains uncertain due to documentation gaps.
    \item Pull request evaluation: Fetch open pull requests and run the same enforcement artifacts on each pull request branch. Compare results against the baseline. Report new violations introduced by each pull request.
    \item Model comparison: Repeat the pipeline using \texttt{gemini-3-flash} and \texttt{claude-sonnet-4-6} for generation. Use \texttt{claude-opus-4-7} for review. Compare the resulting enforcement suites and fix histories.
\end{enumerate}

\subsubsection{SME Data Incorporated}
The solution incorporates ground truth architecture documentation from the ArchAgent benchmark, package-level structural data extracted from each repository, and pull request branches used to evaluate drift introduced by proposed changes.

\section{Justification}
The technical motivation for this solution stems from a critical gap in the current architecture recovery landscape. As highlighted by Pan et al.~\cite{pan2026}, LLMs suffer from local scope bias and cannot hold an entire system dependency matrix in their context window. ArchAgent demonstrates that accurate recovery is achievable despite these limitations. Recovered architectures still lack an enforcement mechanism. Without continuous validation, the same development pressures that caused drift will degrade the recovered architecture again.

This solution makes sense because it shifts architectural governance from a reactive diagnosis to a proactive safeguard. Prior work shows that LLMs are not reliable judges of architectural conformance. They can be accurate translators when given ground truth constraints. This pipeline uses an LLM to translate documentation into executable constraints. Deterministic tools then evaluate the code. ArchUnit serves as the judge for Java repositories. arch-go serves as the judge for Go repositories. This separation reduces ambiguity and removes hallucination risk from the pass or fail decision.

The pipeline also includes cross model validation. A reviewer model audits the generated rules against the same documentation. This adversarial review targets missing constraints, incorrect mappings, and rules that would pass vacuously. The review results are applied as patches and recorded in the fix history.

Model comparison strengthens the justification. Rule generation is evaluated using \texttt{gemini-3-flash} and \texttt{claude-sonnet-4-6}. \texttt{claude-opus-4-7} is used as the reviewer model. This shows whether smaller generation models can still produce enforceable constraints when paired with deterministic enforcement and an adversarial review step.

\section{Design and Implementation}
\subsubsection{Type of Tool}
\begin{itemize}
    \item IDE based workflow driven by prompt templates
    \item Command line enforcement checks that run during validation
    \item Repository scoped enforcement artifacts that can be run locally or in CI
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection{Technologies}
\begin{itemize}
    \item Development environment
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Cursor IDE
    \end{itemize}
    \item Enforcement libraries
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Java: ArchUnit
        \item Go: arch-go
    \end{itemize}
    \item Execution harnesses
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Java: standalone Maven harness
        \item Go: standalone Makefile harness that runs \texttt{arch-go check}
    \end{itemize}
    \item Source control
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Git
    \end{itemize}
    \item Models
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Generation models: \texttt{gemini-3-flash} and \texttt{claude-sonnet-4-6}
        \item Reviewer model: \texttt{claude-opus-4-7}
    \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection{Architecture}
\begin{itemize}
    \item Inputs
    \begin{itemize}
        \item ArchAgent ground truth architecture documentation
        \item Extracted package structure for the target repository
    \end{itemize}
    \item Generation and correction
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Generate Java ArchUnit tests or Go arch-go rules
        \item Fix compilation failures and record changes in the fix history
        \item Review rules against the same documentation and apply patches
    \end{itemize}
    \item Validation and evaluation
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Run against a baseline and triage violations until mapping errors reach zero
        \item Produce a final thoughts calibration report
        \item Run on pull request branches and report new drift
    \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection{Interaction Mechanism}
\begin{enumerate}
    \item The ArchAgent documentation and extracted package structure are provided as prompt inputs
    \item The generation prompt is executed to produce enforcement artifacts
    \item The compilation fix prompt is applied when builds fail
    \item The review prompt is executed and the resulting patches are integrated
    \item The violation analysis prompt is iterated until mapping errors reach zero
    \item The final thoughts prompt is executed for output calibration
    \item The artifacts are deployed on pull request branches and validated against the baseline
\end{enumerate}

\section{Limitations}
\begin{itemize}
    \item Inputs
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Incomplete coverage: The ground truth documentation does not specify every package and dependency present in the current codebase.
        \item Ambiguity: Some constraints are described at a high level, so mapping to concrete packages requires judgment.
        \item Staleness: The documentation may lag behind the current repository version, so some violations may reflect documentation drift rather than code drift.
        \item Granularity mismatch: The documentation may define subsystems while enforcement operates on packages and import paths.
    \end{itemize}
    \item Outputs
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Over strict or over permissive rules: Generated constraints can block legitimate dependencies or miss real drift.
        \item Vacuous constraints risk: Rules can execute successfully while failing to meaningfully constrain the architecture.
        \item LLM hallucination: Generated artifacts can reference functions, classes, or configuration fields that do not exist.
        \item Mapping sensitivity: Incorrect package mappings can produce outputs that enforce the wrong boundaries.
        \item Fragility to refactors: Package restructuring can require rule updates even when architectural intent is unchanged.
        \item Execution scope limits: The checks only cover the code included in the configured build and scan scope.
    \end{itemize}
    \item Systems and datasets
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Scope of evaluated systems: The replication package contains completed artifacts for Spring Framework, Apache ZooKeeper, and HashiCorp Consul only.
        \item Documentation dependency: The approach assumes the availability of ArchAgent benchmark ground truth documentation for each target repository.
    \end{itemize}
    \item Language support
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Supported languages: Java and Go
        \item Not supported: Other languages and enforcement frameworks are out of scope in this implementation.
    \end{itemize}
    \item Evaluation limitations
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Cost and API usage: The workflow depends on paid IDE access and constrained API usage.
        \item Pull request availability: Drift detection depends on the availability and representativeness of open pull requests.
        \item Manual judgment: Some mapping decisions require manual review when documentation is ambiguous or outdated.
    \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

\section{Methodology}
\subsection{Evaluation Goal}
To demonstrate that ground truth architecture documentation can be translated into enforceable build time constraints. The constraints are executed as deterministic checks for Java and Go repositories. The evaluation measures whether the checks detect drift in pull requests before merge.

\subsection{Research Questions}
\begin{enumerate}
    \item RQ1: Can LLMs translate ground truth architecture documentation into correct and executable enforcement artifacts for Java and Go repositories?
    \item RQ2: Do the generated enforcement artifacts detect new architectural violations in open pull requests when compared against a baseline run on the main branch?
    \item RQ3: For rule generation, how do \texttt{gemini-3-flash} and \texttt{claude-sonnet-4-6} compare in correctness and effort when paired with a \texttt{claude-opus-4-7} reviewer model?
\end{enumerate}

\subsection{Success Criteria}
    \begin{enumerate}
    \item RQ1 will be considered positively answered if the generated artifacts execute successfully. The Java artifacts must compile and run under the Maven harness. The Go artifacts must run under the Makefile harness and arch-go. Mapping errors must be reduced to zero in the triage loop.
    \item RQ2 will be considered positively answered if the artifacts detect at least one new violation in an open pull request that is not present in the baseline. If no open pull requests trigger violations, the pipeline must detect a deliberately introduced violation in a controlled fork.
    \item RQ3 will be considered positively answered if \texttt{gemini-3-flash} and \texttt{claude-sonnet-4-6} both reach a stable enforcement suite. The comparison uses the same reviewer model and the same target repositories. The outcome is reported using the resulting artifacts and fix histories.
\end{enumerate}

\subsection{Datasets}
\begin{itemize}
    \item Java repositories
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Spring Framework
        \item Apache ZooKeeper
    \end{itemize}
    \item Go repositories
    \begin{itemize}
        \item HashiCorp Consul
    \end{itemize}
    \item Ground truth architecture documentation from the ArchAgent benchmark for each repository
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Metrics}
\begin{itemize}
    \item Execution success: Whether Java artifacts compile and run under Maven, and whether Go artifacts run under Make and arch-go.
    \item Mapping error count: The number of violation patterns classified as mapping errors at each triage iteration until the count reaches zero.
    \item Cross model catch rate: The number of missing or incorrect constraints identified by the reviewer model during adversarial review.
    \item Pull request drift detection: The number of new violations in pull request branches that are not present in the baseline.
    \item Effort and convergence: The number of compilation and review fixes recorded in the fix history before reaching a stable suite.
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Procedures}
\begin{enumerate}
    \item Select a target repository and obtain the ArchAgent ground truth documentation.
    \item Extract the repository package structure used for mapping constraints to packages and import paths.
    \item Generate enforcement artifacts using the generation prompt.
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Java repositories produce ArchUnit artifacts executed using the Maven harness.
        \item Go repositories produce arch-go artifacts executed using the Makefile harness and \texttt{arch-go check}.
    \end{itemize}
    \item Resolve build failures using the compilation fix prompt. Record each change in the fix history.
    \item Run adversarial review using \texttt{claude-opus-4-7}. Apply review patches and record changes in the fix history.
    \item Establish a baseline by executing the artifacts on the main branch and recording violations.
    \item Evaluate open pull requests by checking out each pull request branch and executing the same artifacts. Record new violations that are not present in the baseline.
    \item Repeat the procedure for model comparison using \texttt{gemini-3-flash} and \texttt{claude-sonnet-4-6} as generation models while keeping \texttt{claude-opus-4-7} as the reviewer model.
\end{enumerate}

\section{Conclusions and future work}
\begin{itemize}
    \item Conclusions
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \due{Explicit conclusions from the project}
        \item \due{Lessons learned about enforcement quality and drift detection}
    \end{itemize}
    \item Future work
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \due{Future research activities to improve documentation alignment and rule robustness}
        \item \due{Future evaluation activities such as more repositories, more PRs, and additional languages}
    \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}
